questions.question: What is the recovery operation for a detected X error on qubit i?
questions.explanation: Since X² = I, applying X again reverses the original X error. The recovery operation is the same as the error because Pauli operators are their own inverses.

questions.question: When is error correction successful?

options.text: When the syndrome is all zeros
When R_s × E is in the stabilizer group
When R_s × E is a logical operator

questions.explanation: Recovery succeeds when the combined operation R_s × E (recovery times error) is in the stabilizer group S. Stabilizers act as identity on the code space, so the state is restored.

questions.question: What does it mean for a code to be degenerate?

options.text: Different errors can have the same syndrome and same effect

questions.explanation: A degenerate code has multiple errors with the same syndrome where E₁†E₂ is a stabilizer. This means both errors have the same effect on the code space, so the same correction works for both.

questions.question: What is a lookup table decoder?

options.text: A decoder that searches for minimum weight errors
A pre-computed mapping from syndromes to corrections
A neural network for error correction
A decoder that uses quantum search

questions.explanation: A lookup table decoder pre-computes the syndrome → correction mapping for all correctable errors. At runtime, it simply looks up the syndrome in the table to find the correction, giving O(1) decoding time.

questions.question: What happens if the recovery operation R_s × E is a logical operator?

options.text: The state returns to code space
A logical error occurs - encoded data is corrupted

questions.explanation: If R_s × E ∈ N(S) \ S (a logical operator), it changes the encoded information. The state returns to the code space but the logical qubit is flipped - this is a logical error.

questions.question: What is the error correction threshold?

options.text: Physical error rate below which larger codes reduce logical error rate

questions.explanation: The threshold p_th is the physical error rate below which increasing code size reduces the logical error rate. For p < p_th, error correction provides a net benefit; for p > p_th, larger codes make things worse.

questions.question: In a degenerate code, applying the wrong correction (for a different error with the same syndrome) always causes a logical error.
questions.explanation: False. In degenerate codes, different errors with the same syndrome may have the same effect on the code space (their product is a stabilizer). In this case, any of the corresponding corrections works correctly.
